CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper is generally well-organized and readable with a logical progression from motivation through algorithm (MC-IC), dataset creation, and experiments; main contributions are stated early and related work coverage is thorough. Notation appears consistent and hyperparameters are reported, and figures/tables (including human evaluation and model comparisons) are informative for the claims about decoy generation and multitask trade-offs. Minor issues remain: human rater disagreement is relegated to supplementary material and code availability is not mentioned, which slightly hampers reproducibility details in the presentation. Overall the writing quality is good with only minor presentation gaps. | B(4): Overall the paper is well organized and the main contributions are stated early (decoy-generation mechanism, MCIC dataset, human evaluation and baseline models), making the flow from method to experiments logical. Notation appears consistent and writing quality is good, with figures/tables and examples effectively supporting the narrative. Minor clarity issues remain around the human evaluation protocol and discussion of raters’ disagreement (noted as in supplemental), which could be brought into the main text for stronger presentation. From a vision-language reviewer perspective the paper is a solid, readable contribution with only modest presentation improvements needed.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper is generally well-organized and readable with a logical progression from motivation through algorithm (MC-IC), dataset creation, and experiments; main contributions are stated early and related work coverage is thorough. Notation appears consistent and hyperparameters are reported, and figures/tables (including human evaluation and model comparisons) are informative for the claims about decoy generation and multitask trade-offs. Minor issues remain: human rater disagreement is relegated to supplementary material and code availability is not mentioned, which slightly hampers reproducibility details in the presentation. Overall the writing quality is good with only minor presentation gaps.

reviewer_b.justification: Overall the paper is well organized and the main contributions are stated early (decoy-generation mechanism, MCIC dataset, human evaluation and baseline models), making the flow from method to experiments logical. Notation appears consistent and writing quality is good, with figures/tables and examples effectively supporting the narrative. From a vision-language reviewer perspective the paper is a solid, readable contribution with only modest presentation improvements needed.

SUBSTANCE.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(3): The paper provides a range of models, human upper bounds, and a large MCIC dataset generated with a described decoy algorithm, plus reported hyperparameters and a multitask analysis showing the detection–generation tradeoff. However, important reproducibility elements appear missing (code release not mentioned, unclear seed/split details) and sensitivity analyses are limited — e.g., more ablations of the decoy-generation components, parameter sweeps, and statistical significance/robustness across subsets are not reported. Related work is well covered and limitations (human rater disagreement, naive decoy issues) are discussed, but the experimental depth is adequate rather than fully comprehensive. | B(4): The paper provides substantial experimental evidence: a large-scale MCIC dataset creation method, human upper-bound evaluation, multiple neural baselines (FFNN, Vec2seq, Vec2seq+FFNN) and analyses showing correlation between comprehension and captioning performance. They include sensitivity analysis via the λgen trade-off, report hyperparameters, and discuss key limitations (decoy selection, human disagreement). Coverage of related work appears thorough, but reproducibility is somewhat weakened by no mention of released code and ablations beyond λgen are limited, so the study is solid with minor gaps.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper provides a range of models, human upper bounds, and a large MCIC dataset generated with a described decoy algorithm, plus reported hyperparameters and a multitask analysis showing the detection–generation tradeoff. However, important reproducibility elements appear missing (code release not mentioned, unclear seed/split details) and sensitivity analyses are limited — e.g., more ablations of the decoy-generation components, parameter sweeps, and statistical significance/robustness across subsets are not reported. Related work is well covered and limitations (human rater disagreement, naive decoy issues) are discussed, but the experimental depth is adequate rather than fully comprehensive.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper provides substantial experimental evidence: a large-scale MCIC dataset creation method, human upper-bound evaluation, multiple neural baselines (FFNN, Vec2seq, Vec2seq+FFNN) and analyses showing correlation between comprehension and captioning performance. They include sensitivity analysis via the λgen trade-off, report hyperparameters, and discuss key limitations (decoy selection, human disagreement). Coverage of related work appears thorough, but reproducibility is somewhat weakened by no mention of released code and ablations beyond λgen are limited, so the study is solid with minor gaps.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): This paper sits squarely within the Vision+Language multimodal area and addresses a problem (dual machine comprehension with challenging decoys) that is highly relevant to top NLP/ML venues. The methodology (PV+surface-sim decoy generation), large-scale MCIC dataset derived from COCO, human upper-bound evaluation, and standard metrics (accuracy, ROUGE-L, CIDEr, BLEU) match community expectations and the audience. I note some reproducibility concerns (code release not mentioned) and practical evaluation choices (human rater disagreement, decoy construction trade-offs) that should be clarified, but these do not undermine overall venue fit. | B(5): This paper sits squarely within top-tier NLP/ML venues: it proposes a novel decoy-generation mechanism, releases a large-scale MCIC dataset derived from COCO, and provides human upper-bounds and multimodal baselines (FFNN, Vec2seq, Vec2seq+FFNN) with standard metrics (accuracy, ROUGE, CIDEr). The evaluation methodology and emphasis on human vs. model gaps, plus multi-task insights linking comprehension and captioning, will be directly relevant to the vision-and-language research community and the venue’s audience.

reviewer_a.justification: This paper sits squarely within the Vision+Language multimodal area and addresses a problem (dual machine comprehension with challenging decoys) that is highly relevant to top NLP/ML venues. The methodology (PV+surface-sim decoy generation), large-scale MCIC dataset derived from COCO, human upper-bound evaluation, and standard metrics (accuracy, ROUGE-L, CIDEr, BLEU) match community expectations and the audience. I note some reproducibility concerns (code release not mentioned) and practical evaluation choices (human rater disagreement, decoy construction trade-offs) that should be clarified, but these do not undermine overall venue fit.

reviewer_b.justification: This paper sits squarely within top-tier NLP/ML venues: it proposes a novel decoy-generation mechanism, releases a large-scale MCIC dataset derived from COCO, and provides human upper-bounds and multimodal baselines (FFNN, Vec2seq, Vec2seq+FFNN) with standard metrics (accuracy, ROUGE, CIDEr). The evaluation methodology and emphasis on human vs. model gaps, plus multi-task insights linking comprehension and captioning, will be directly relevant to the vision-and-language research community and the venue’s audience.

ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): The paper offers a useful, practical contribution by proposing an algorithm that combines paragraph-vector semantic similarity with surface-level BLEU-based similarity to generate challenging decoy captions and by releasing the MCIC dataset derived from COCO. However, both components (PV embeddings, BLEU/surface similarity) and the use of multiple-choice decoys are existing techniques, so the core idea is an incremental combination rather than a fundamentally new method. The authors do clearly differentiate their contribution (automatic decoy generation + large-scale dataset + human upper bounds) and provide empirical baselines, which would inform practitioners but likely would not surprise experts as a major conceptual advance. | B(3): The paper contributes a practical mechanism for generating challenging decoy captions (combining paragraph-vector similarity with a BLEU-based surface-similarity) and creates a large MCIC dataset applied to COCO, which is a useful resource. However, the methodological components (PV embeddings, surface-similarity heuristics, and multitask Vec2seq+FFNN) are largely combinations of existing techniques rather than a fundamentally new modeling paradigm. The authors do clearly differentiate their dataset and decoy-generation procedure from naïve random baselines and provide human upper-bounds, but the overall contribution reads as an incremental, application-focused advance rather than a highly novel theoretical or algorithmic breakthrough.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper offers a useful, practical contribution by proposing an algorithm that combines paragraph-vector semantic similarity with surface-level BLEU-based similarity to generate challenging decoy captions and by releasing the MCIC dataset derived from COCO. However, both components (PV embeddings, BLEU/surface similarity) and the use of multiple-choice decoys are existing techniques, so the core idea is an incremental combination rather than a fundamentally new method. The authors do clearly differentiate their contribution (automatic decoy generation + large-scale dataset + human upper bounds) and provide empirical baselines, which would inform practitioners but likely would not surprise experts as a major conceptual advance.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper contributes a practical mechanism for generating challenging decoy captions (combining paragraph-vector similarity with a BLEU-based surface-similarity) and creates a large MCIC dataset applied to COCO, which is a useful resource. However, the methodological components (PV embeddings, surface-similarity heuristics, and multitask Vec2seq+FFNN) are largely combinations of existing techniques rather than a fundamentally new modeling paradigm. The authors do clearly differentiate their dataset and decoy-generation procedure from naïve random baselines and provide human upper-bounds, but the overall contribution reads as an incremental, application-focused advance rather than a highly novel theoretical or algorithmic breakthrough.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper evaluates several sensible baselines (FFNN, Vec2seq, Vec2seq+FFNN) on the same MCIC/COCO data with consistent metrics (accuracy for detection and standard captioning scores), and discusses limitations and trade-offs (e.g., λgen). However, from the summary it appears important recent multimodal/retrieval or caption-ranking methods are not explicitly compared, and code/reproducibility details (and thus exact parity of training conditions) are not mentioned—these are minor but notable omissions given the task. Overall the comparisons are solid and contextualized, but not fully comprehensive against the strongest competing approaches. | B(4): The paper evaluates reasonable baselines (FFNN, Vec2seq, and a multitask Vec2seq+FFNN) on the MCIC dataset derived from COCO, using consistent metrics (accuracy, ROUGE-L, CIDEr) and reports human upper bounds—so comparisons are generally fair. However, from the provided summary it appears important recent vision–language retrieval and multimodal comprehension models (and alternative decoy-generation strategies) are not directly compared, which would strengthen claims of superiority; the authors do acknowledge some prior simple baselines (random decoys) but do not demonstrate head-to-head results versus the strongest competing approaches. Overall the comparisons are good but miss a few key contemporary baselines and explicit prior-method performance numbers.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper evaluates several sensible baselines (FFNN, Vec2seq, Vec2seq+FFNN) on the same MCIC/COCO data with consistent metrics (accuracy for detection and standard captioning scores), and discusses limitations and trade-offs (e.g., λgen). However, from the summary it appears important recent multimodal/retrieval or caption-ranking methods are not explicitly compared, and code/reproducibility details (and thus exact parity of training conditions) are not mentioned—these are minor but notable omissions given the task. Overall the comparisons are solid and contextualized, but not fully comprehensive against the strongest competing approaches.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper evaluates reasonable baselines (FFNN, Vec2seq, and a multitask Vec2seq+FFNN) on the MCIC dataset derived from COCO, using consistent metrics (accuracy, ROUGE-L, CIDEr) and reports human upper bounds—so comparisons are generally fair. However, from the provided summary it appears important recent vision–language retrieval and multimodal comprehension models (and alternative decoy-generation strategies) are not directly compared, which would strengthen claims of superiority; the authors do acknowledge some prior simple baselines (random decoys) but do not demonstrate head-to-head results versus the strongest competing approaches. Overall the comparisons are good but miss a few key contemporary baselines and explicit prior-method performance numbers.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(3): The paper states clear claims and provides experiments (decoy-generation algorithm applied to COCO producing MCIC, human evaluation, baselines including Vec2seq+FFNN) that generally support those claims, but several methodological issues limit confidence. I see no mention of released code or precise data splits/seed control, and the summary does not report statistical significance, confidence intervals, or robustness analyses—important for establishing that the decoy generation is reliably challenging and that model improvements are meaningful. The mathematical components appear straightforward and no formal proofs were needed, but potential train/test leakage and the noted human rater disagreement suggest incomplete control analyses. Overall the work is promising but missing key reproducibility and statistical rigor to fully substantiate all claims. | B(4): The paper’s central claims (decoy-generation mechanism, MCIC dataset, human upper-bound, and baseline/multitask model evaluations) are supported by experiments and human evaluation as described, and the methodology uses reasonable baselines (FFNN, Vec2seq, hybrid) with reported hyperparameters and metrics. There is no indication of mathematical derivation issues, but the summary does not mention statistical significance testing or ablation depth beyond reporting correlations, which are minor methodological gaps. The authors acknowledge key limitations (decoy scaling, human rater disagreement) and the presented results appear to justify the conclusions, so only minor issues prevent a perfect technical score.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper states clear claims and provides experiments (decoy-generation algorithm applied to COCO producing MCIC, human evaluation, baselines including Vec2seq+FFNN) that generally support those claims, but several methodological issues limit confidence. I see no mention of released code or precise data splits/seed control, and the summary does not report statistical significance, confidence intervals, or robustness analyses—important for establishing that the decoy generation is reliably challenging and that model improvements are meaningful. The mathematical components appear straightforward and no formal proofs were needed, but potential train/test leakage and the noted human rater disagreement suggest incomplete control analyses. Overall the work is promising but missing key reproducibility and statistical rigor to fully substantiate all claims.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper’s central claims (decoy-generation mechanism, MCIC dataset, human upper-bound, and baseline/multitask model evaluations) are supported by experiments and human evaluation as described, and the methodology uses reasonable baselines (FFNN, Vec2seq, hybrid) with reported hyperparameters and metrics. There is no indication of mathematical derivation issues, but the summary does not mention statistical significance testing or ablation depth beyond reporting correlations, which are minor methodological gaps. The authors acknowledge key limitations (decoy scaling, human rater disagreement) and the presented results appear to justify the conclusions, so only minor issues prevent a perfect technical score.

IMPACT.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): The paper addresses a meaningful problem—constructing challenging multiple-choice image-caption pairs and quantifying human upper-bounds—by proposing an extensible decoy-generation algorithm and a large MCIC dataset derived from COCO, which is practically useful for vision+language evaluation. The provided baselines (FFNN, Vec2seq, Vec2seq+FFNN) and the observed positive correlation between comprehension accuracy and captioning performance lend empirical value, but the contribution is incremental rather than transformative: novelty is mainly in dataset/construction strategy and not a radically new modeling paradigm. Impact is likely moderate—researchers working on multimodal evaluation and captioning will reuse the dataset and build on the decoy-generation approach, but broader field-wide shifts are unlikely without released code and stronger evidence of generalization beyond COCO. | B(3): The paper addresses an important and practical problem—creating challenging multimodal multiple-choice data and human upper bounds—by proposing a scalable decoy-generation method and releasing the large MCIC dataset built from COCO. Its combination of PV and surface-similarity to generate hard negatives and the reported human vs. model gap make it a useful benchmark and the provided baselines (FFNN, Vec2seq, Vec2seq+FFNN) plus the observed correlation between comprehension and captioning performance will likely guide follow-up work. However, the contribution is evolutionary rather than transformative: it supplies a valuable resource and evaluation protocol that will be adopted and extended, but does not by itself fundamentally change how researchers think about vision-language modeling.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper addresses a meaningful problem—constructing challenging multiple-choice image-caption pairs and quantifying human upper-bounds—by proposing an extensible decoy-generation algorithm and a large MCIC dataset derived from COCO, which is practically useful for vision+language evaluation. The provided baselines (FFNN, Vec2seq, Vec2seq+FFNN) and the observed positive correlation between comprehension accuracy and captioning performance lend empirical value, but the contribution is incremental rather than transformative: novelty is mainly in dataset/construction strategy and not a radically new modeling paradigm. Impact is likely moderate—researchers working on multimodal evaluation and captioning will reuse the dataset and build on the decoy-generation approach, but broader field-wide shifts are unlikely without released code and stronger evidence of generalization beyond COCO.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper addresses an important and practical problem—creating challenging multimodal multiple-choice data and human upper bounds—by proposing a scalable decoy-generation method and releasing the large MCIC dataset built from COCO. Its combination of PV and surface-similarity to generate hard negatives and the reported human vs. model gap make it a useful benchmark and the provided baselines (FFNN, Vec2seq, Vec2seq+FFNN) plus the observed correlation between comprehension and captioning performance will likely guide follow-up work. However, the contribution is evolutionary rather than transformative: it supplies a valuable resource and evaluation protocol that will be adopted and extended, but does not by itself fundamentally change how researchers think about vision-language modeling.

main_claims: An effective and extensible mechanism for generating challenging decoy captions from human-created image captions.
A large-scale Dual Machine Comprehension dataset (MCIC) constructed by applying the decoy-generation algorithm to the COCO dataset, which the authors make publicly available.
Human evaluation results on the MCIC dataset establishing a human performance upper-bound.

Baseline and neural models (including FFNN, Vec2seq, and Vec2seq+FFNN) are provided and evaluated, showing that performance on the DMC task positively correlates with image captioning performance in a multi-task setting.
_summary.methodology: Automatically generate difficult decoy captions by combining paragraph-vector (PV) embedding similarity with linguistic surface similarity (simSURF computed via BLEU), apply this MC-IC algorithm to COCO to create MCIC, and evaluate models (baseline and neural multitask Vec2seq+FFNN) on detection accuracy and caption generation metrics.
baselines: FFNN (feedforward neural network using Inception-v3 and paragraph-vector features)
CNN image features from GoogleInception-v3 + paragraph-vector caption embeddings
metrics: Accuracy (detecting the true caption among decoys)
limitations: Naïve selection of random decoy captions is overly simplistic and can make the dataset too easy.
It is impractical to increase the number of decoys to raise difficulty because of human evaluation burden.
There is an observed trade-off between comprehension (detection) accuracy and generation performance when tuning λgen.
Human raters exhibit disagreement (discussed further in supplementary material).
_summary.topic_area: Vision+Language — multimodal machine comprehension / image captioning

reviewer_a.name: Experimental Methodologist
reviewer_a.background: Senior ML engineer with 8+ years building and evaluating multimodal systems, responsible for benchmarking, dataset curation, and reproducible pipelines in industry and academia.
traits: checks for rigorous experimental controls, clear baselines, and appropriate ablation studies
values reproducibility: requests release of code, seed control, training/validation splits, and hyperparameter details
examines statistical validity: looks for significance testing, confidence intervals, and robustness across subsets
insists on clear metric definitions and consistent evaluation protocols (e.g., train/test leakage, candidate sampling)
reviewer_a.reviewing_style: Practical, detail-oriented reviewer who focuses on whether the experiments support the claims and whether the work can be reliably reproduced and compared.

reviewer_b.name: Vision-Language Researcher
reviewer_b.background: Associate professor specializing in vision-and-language research, with a track record of dataset construction, human evaluation, and modeling advances in image captioning and multimodal comprehension.
traits: assesses novelty and positioning relative to prior datasets and decoy-generation methods
values the dataset's potential impact on the field: long-term usefulness, diversity, and suitability for downstream tasks
looks for clear human evaluation methodology and meaningful interpretation of human vs. model performance gaps
notices whether proposed modeling contributions advance understanding or are incremental engineering
reviewer_b.reviewing_style: Contextual reviewer who evaluates the paper's novelty, dataset and model significance, and whether it moves the vision-language field forward.
